question: What happens if you don't check the return value of writable.write()?

options.text: Data accumulates in memory, potentially causing OOM crash
Node.js automatically pauses the readable
options.explanation: Automatic pausing only works when using pipe() or pipeline(). With manual write() calls, you must check the return value and call pause() yourself

options.text: write() throws an exception
options.explanation: write() never throws on buffer overflow — it returns false. The data will still be written, but the buffer will grow uncontrollably

options.text: Data will be lost
options.explanation: Data is not lost — it all goes into the Writable stream buffer. The problem is the buffer grows without limits and can exhaust memory

question: Which way of reading a Readable Stream is recommended in modern Node.js?

options.text: for await (const chunk of readable)
readable.on('data', callback)
options.explanation: The data event works, but async iterator automatically handles end, error, and backpressure. It's a cleaner and safer approach

options.text: readable.on('readable', () => readable.read())
options.explanation: Paused mode with read() gives full control, but in most cases async iterator is simpler and safer

options.text: readable.pipe(writable)
options.explanation: pipe() is convenient for redirecting to a Writable, but for data processing async iterator is preferable

question: How does pipeline() differ from pipe()?

options.text: pipeline() automatically destroys all streams on error
pipeline() supports AbortSignal for cancellation
pipeline() has callback/promise API for tracking completion
pipeline() is faster than pipe()
options.explanation: Data transfer speed is the same. pipeline() adds safety (error handling, stream destruction), not performance

question: What is objectMode for in a Transform stream?

options.text: To pass JS objects instead of Buffer/string through the stream
To automatically serialize objects to JSON
options.explanation: objectMode doesn't serialize data — it simply allows push() and read() to work with arbitrary JS objects instead of Buffer

options.text: To improve performance when working with objects
options.explanation: objectMode doesn't affect performance. It changes the data type in the stream: JS objects instead of Buffer/string

options.text: To automatically validate data
options.explanation: objectMode doesn't add validation. It only allows passing arbitrary JS values through the stream

question: What do cork() and uncork() do in a Writable stream?

options.text: cork() buffers all write() calls in memory, uncork() sends everything in a single system call
cork() pauses the stream, uncork() resumes it
options.explanation: cork/uncork is a write optimization (batching), not flow control. For pause/resume, use pause()/resume()

options.text: cork() encrypts data, uncork() decrypts it
options.explanation: cork/uncork have nothing to do with encryption. For encryption, use crypto.createCipheriv()

options.text: cork() compresses data, uncork() decompresses it
options.explanation: cork/uncork have nothing to do with compression. For compression, use zlib (createGzip, createDeflate)
